coredata.dc:title: Acoustic cavitation assisted plasma for wastewater treatment: Degradation of Rhodamine B in aqueous solution
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Yu
Hariu, Daiki
Yamamoto, Takuya
Komarov, Sergey
coredata.dc:description: A novel wastewater treatment process, acoustic cavitation assisted plasma (ACAP) is proposed in this study aiming at expanding the treatable range of water pollutants due to a synergetic effect of ultrasound irradiation and high voltage plasma discharge. In this process, the role of acoustic cavitation is not only to provide generation of chemically active OH radicals, as for example in conventional ultrasonic wastewater treatment techniques, but also to ensure conditions for stable plasma generation in wastewater and, thus, to extend the treatable range of water pollutants. Rhodamine B (RhB) was used as a model pollutant in experiments examining effects of ultrasound amplitude, RhB initial concentration, output voltage, solution pH and electrical conductivity on the RhB degradation efficiency. The results revealed that the ultrasound-assisted plasma generation requires lower output voltages and allows to increase the acceptable range of electrical conductivity of treatable solutions up to 1000 μS/cm, that is about 24 times higher than in the case of conventional plasma discharge treatment. The alkaline and acid medium were found to be favorable for higher degradation efficiency. Additional measurements and results of recent investigations concerning underwater plasma showed that microbubbles presented in cavitation zone could serve as “bridges” making the pulse discharge propagation between the electrodes easier than in the conventional case. Besides, acoustic cavitation assists a faster transition of plasma discharge from ineffective streamer type to more effective spark type that further contributes to the improvement of the treatment performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acoustic cavitation
Liquid plasma
Rhodamine B degradation
Microbubble
Spark discharge

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Expanded available range of plasma generation
Effects of pH on RhB degradation
Effects of solution conductivity on RhB degradation
Effects of output voltage on RhB degradation
Mechanism of breakdown in ACAP process
Synergistic effects of the ACAP process

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Expanded available pH and conductivity range in the ACAP process.
Mechanism of plasma propagation with and without acoustic cavitation.
The semi-logarithm graph of RhB concentration versus treatment time at various initial concentrations.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of Rhodamine B degradation in ACAP process for a 30 min treatment (5 mg/L initial concentration, 25 kV output voltage, pH 5, 20 µS/cm electric conductivity).
Degradation efficiency and reaction rate constant as functions of initial concentration.
Degradation efficiency and reaction rate constant as functions of solution pH.
Degradation efficiency and reaction rate constant as functions of solution conductivity.
Degradation efficiency and reaction rate constant as functions of output voltage.
Photos of spark discharge through a bubble chain taken by high speed camera (frame rate: 3000 fps, shutter speed: 1/15,000 s).
Photos of spark discharge through bubbles near needle electrode taken by high speed camera (frame rate: 10,000 fps, shutter speed: 1/20,000 s).
Photos of spark discharge with bubble contact taken by high speed camera (frame rate: 10,000 fps, shutter speed: 1/35,000 s).
Voltage and current of plasma discharge without acoustic cavitation.
Voltage and current of plasma discharge with acoustic cavitation.
Comparison of degradation efficiency by using plasma and ultrasound alone, as well as ACAP process.
Comparisons of degradation rate constant (min−1) in various processes.
Spark discharge frequency in solutions with various conductivities (μS/cm).
Average degradation rate and reaction constant of experiments with various parameters.

head.ce:title: Acoustic cavitation assisted plasma for wastewater treatment: Degradation of Rhodamine B in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School of Environmental Studies, Tohoku University, 6-6-02 Aza Aoba, Aramaki, Aoba-Ku, Sendai 980-8579, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel wastewater treatment process, acoustic cavitation assisted plasma (ACAP) is proposed in this study aiming at expanding the treatable range of water pollutants due to a synergetic effect of ultrasound irradiation and high voltage plasma discharge. In this process, the role of acoustic cavitation is not only to provide generation of chemically active OH radicals, as for example in conventional ultrasonic wastewater treatment techniques, but also to ensure conditions for stable plasma generation in wastewater and, thus, to extend the treatable range of water pollutants. Rhodamine B (RhB) was used as a model pollutant in experiments examining effects of ultrasound amplitude, RhB initial concentration, output voltage, solution pH and electrical conductivity on the RhB degradation efficiency. The results revealed that the ultrasound-assisted plasma generation requires lower output voltages and allows to increase the acceptable range of electrical conductivity of treatable solutions up to 1000 μS/cm, that is about 24 times higher than in the case of conventional plasma discharge treatment. The alkaline and acid medium were found to be favorable for higher degradation efficiency. Additional measurements and results of recent investigations concerning underwater plasma showed that microbubbles presented in cavitation zone could serve as “bridges” making the pulse discharge propagation between the electrodes easier than in the conventional case. Besides, acoustic cavitation assists a faster transition of plasma discharge from ineffective streamer type to more effective spark type that further contributes to the improvement of the treatment performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have been noticed in recent years as innovative tools for wastewater treatment. With the introduction of energy (chemical, electrical and radiative) into the reaction zone, highly reactive species can be generated to attack and degrade pollutant molecules [1,2]. Electrical plasma discharge, as one of the AOPs, shows high removal efficiency and environmental compatibility for treating organic pollutants [3]. Electrical plasma discharge leads to extreme physical conditions, which generate radicals (such as H, O, and OH), UV light, shockwave and exert a direct pyrolysis effect on the pollutant molecules [4–6]. Consequently, electrical plasma discharge can be used alone as well as in combination with other AOPs techniques, such as UV photocatalysis and ozonation. However, it is usually required much higher voltage to breakdown the water medium between electrodes as compared to air. Also, the underwater plasma discharge is very sensitive to the environment, for example to wastewater chemical composition. The plasma discharge can occur in two different mechanisms, namely spark and streamer mechanism, that greatly affects the organic removal efficiency in water treatment [7,8]. Although streamer type discharge has some advantages for UV irradiation and intense plasma channels, much energy is consumed for Joule heating instead of pollutant degradation, especially in the case of refractory organic pollutants [9–13]. These demerits have hindered applications of conventional plasma processes. Although some efforts have been made, such as combination with bubbling into the reaction zone, or using various electrode arrangements [14–19], generation of stable discharge plasma in aqueous solutions still remains very challenging. Therefore, effective removal of pollutants is unachievable in many cases, especially in industrial applications.

Ultrasound has been widely investigated also as an AOP technique for wastewater treatment, medicine, food and mineral processing. When ultrasound is irradiated into a liquid, acoustic cavitation occurs. This is a physical phenomenon which includes nucleation, growth, and implosion of vapor or gas-filled cavities. According to the “hot spot” theory these cavities, or cavitation microbubbles, generate high pressure (up to 1000 atm) and high temperature (up to 6000 K) in a small volume [20–22], and as a result, a tremendous amount of highly reactive radicals are produced in the cavitation zone. Besides, due to the absorption of ultrasound waves in cavitating liquid, a macro steady fluid streaming is also generated. This phenomenon, called acoustic streaming, can greatly improve the mass transfer inside the reactor [23]. Enhancement in the efficiency of several AOPs by ultrasound technique has been reported. They include but not limited to the combination of ultrasound irradiation with ozonation, Fenton reaction, and photocatalysis. This is because the treatment efficiency of ultrasound is insufficient when used singly [24–27]. Few studies [28,29] have been conducted on combination of ultrasound and underwater plasma, however, the size of experimental reactors was very limited in these researches. Moreover, effects of ultrasound on underwater plasma were not discussed properly.

In the present paper a novel technique, acoustic cavitation assisted plasma (ACAP) has been proposed aiming at improving the pollutant removal efficiency in wastewater. The key point of this technique is combining the two advanced oxidation processes, and thus utilizing numerous microbubbles generated during acoustic cavitation. This allows to significantly expand the range of plasma treatable wastewater regarding its chemical composition and physical properties. Particularly, in this study effects of acoustic cavitation on enhanced plasma treatment ability was investigated first under various pH and electrical conductivity of aqueous solutions. Then, in order to evaluate the performance of ACAP reactor, a highly soluble and non-volatile organic compound, Rhodamine B (RhB) was chosen as a model pollutant to experimentally investigate its degradation efficiency under different conditions of plasma and ultrasonic irradiation. Then, the possible mechanisms of synergistic effects were discussed based on the experimental observations.

The experimental setup, as shown in Fig. 1 , consists of an ultrasound generation system, ACAP reactor, plasma generation circuit, an electrical analytical system and cooling device. An ultrasound generator (TELSONIC, Switzerland) with adjustable vibration amplitude is applied to irradiate ultrasound waves at a frequency of 20 kHz into a water bath through a cylindrical barbell sonotrode connected with a piezoceramic transducer. The peak-to-peak amplitude of the sonotrode tip is ranged from 40 to 75 μm (p-p), the diameter of working tip is 48 mm. Notice that the threshold vibration amplitude exceeding which causes developed cavitation in water for this sonotrode is 4–5 μm (p-p) [30]. Therefore, a zone of developed cavitation is formed under the sonotrode tip in the present experiments as shown in Figs. 1 and 3. The ACAP reactor comprises of the above sonotrode, a needle shape high voltage electrode made of tungsten wire (Diam. 1 mm), and a cylindrical vessel (Diam. 140 mm, height 190 mm) made of acrylic resin. The sonotrode is composed of two cylindrical parts, one is made of ceramic to prevent the ultrasound generator from high voltage damage, and the other one is made of titanium serving as the sonotrode tip and grounded electrode. The lengths of the ceramic and titanium parts are 221 and 244 mm, respectively. Dimensions of both parts have been adjusted to resonance conditions. The distance between electrodes is an important parameter influencing the plasma discharge efficiency. In this research, the distance is set to 4 mm according to preliminary experiments in optimizing the performance of spark type discharge unit.

Plasma generation high voltage circuit is showed in Fig. 1 by the blue line. A DC power supply (0 ∼ +40 kV) is used to charge a capacitor (1000 pF), and then it is discharged into the ASAP reactor through a spark gap discharge unit. The discharge frequency is measured to be 50–70 Hz. Voltage and current signals between the electrode during the plasma discharge are collected by an oscilloscope through a 1000:1 reduction ratio high voltage probe (PINTEC, China) and a coil current probe (IWATSU, Japan) respectively. A water-cooling coil is submerged in the ACAP reactor to maintain the water temperature at a level of 20 ± 2 °C.

High purity RhB was purchased (Wako, Japan) and various RhB concentrations solutions, from 2.5 to 10 mg/L, were prepared using distilled water. Each experiment of RhB degradation lasted for 12 min, and solution samples were taken each 3 min. RhB concentration was determined from the absorbance measured by a spectrophotometer (AS-ONE ASV11D, Japan) at 554 nm wavelength. After the treatment, the RhB degradation efficiency η, was calculated as follows:
η =
R h B
0
-
R h B
R h B
0
where
R h B
is the concentration of RhB after the treatment,
R h B
0
is the initial concentration. The solution pH was adjusted with NaOH and H2SO4 buffer solutions, and conductivity was adjusted using a NaCl solution.

A high-speed camera (FASTCAM 1024PCI, Photoron Ltd.) was used to record the plasma discharge between the electrodes. The record area is indicated in Fig. 1 by the purple dash line. The frame rate of each photograph was ranged from 3000 to 10,000 fps. The color of the taken photographs was grey-scaled. Additionally, a halogen lamp was used in these experiments as a back light to observe the bubble movement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Expanded available range of plasma generation
ce:section.ce:para:
In this part, the solution pH and conductivity ranges for plasma generation were adjusted respectively to evaluate the effects of acoustic cavitation. Besides, the effect of ultrasound amplitude was also investigated. In all experiments, the circuit output voltage was 25 kV, and the appearance of the spark discharge between electrodes was regarded as a breakdown event at a given pH and conductivity. Fig. 2
indicates that acoustic cavitation significantly expanded the possible range for plasma generation in water. Without acoustic cavitation, spark discharge breakdown occurs only when the solution conductivity is lower than 39.9 μS/cm. On the other hand, with the assistance of acoustic cavitation, the conductivity upper limit for breakdown reached 1002 μS/cm, that is about 24 times higher than that without acoustic cavitation. However, pH had a negligibly small effect on the plasma breakdown regardless of the acoustic cavitation. As for ultrasound amplitude, no obvious effect at higher amplitude was observed. That maybe because even under the smallest ultrasound amplitude examined in this study, the cavitation intensity was very high and probably close to saturation level. Therefore, any further increase in the vibration amplitude had no effect on the cavitation characteristics.

The reason why the ultrasound irradiation makes it possible to expand the range of conductivity is that the existence of numerous cavitation bubbles between electrodes makes the plasma discharge easier to propagate comparing with that without cavitation. This is schematically shown in Fig. 3. The results of recent researches on plasma discharge with a single bubble in water [31,32] reveal that the highest electrical field is located in the immediate vicinity of the bubble interface making it easier to generate electrical discharge. Moreover, the bubble interface takes an important role in the discharge propagation. For the discharge occurred near the needle tip, there are a few possible pathways to “choose” its propagation to the ground electrode. Because of the low electron mobility in water, the charge accumulates on the surface of bubbles. In the above-cited studies, the bubble diameters were ranged within 2–8 mm. In the ACAP process, a huge amount of cavitation bubbles of much smaller diameters (tens of microns) provide a much greater surface area and some bubbles can serve as “bridges” when the plasma is discharged between electrodes. Notice that the role of microbubbles in an electrical pulse breakdown of water has been discussed in the literature [33].

From the application point of view, it is important to investigate the dependence of removal efficiency on the initial concentration of the substance to be treated. In this part, effect of RhB concentration was investigated within a range of 2.5–10 mg/L. The output voltage of the power supply was 25 kV, pH and conductivity were adjusted to 5 and 200 μS/cm, respectively. It is known that the degradation of Rhodamine B by AOPs is a pseudo first order reaction [34–36]. Therefore, kinetics of RhB degradation can be written as follows:
ln
R h B
0
R h B
= k t
where
R h B
is the concentration of RhB at the time t and k is the degradation rate constant (min−1). The semi-logarithm graph of RhB concentration for various initial concentrations versus treatment time (Fig. 4 ) yields a straight line, that confirms the pseudo first order kinetics of RhB degradation in the ACAP process. Fig. 5 shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra measured every 6 min during a 30 min RhB degradation experiment with the initial concentration of 5 mg/L.

The degradation efficiency and degradation rate constant versus initial RhB concentration are shown in Fig. 6 . Both the degradation efficiency and reaction constant decreased with increasing the initial concentration. It has been reported that the concentration of OH radicals is remarkably higher at the surface of collapsed acoustic bubbles and vapor bubbles formed by plasma [37,38]. Because of the short life time (3.7 × 10−9 s) and short diffusion distance (approximately 6 × 10−9 m) of OH radicals, they can attack the RhB molecules and decompose them only at the bubble-liquid interface [3,39]. Therefore, molecules of RhB or their degraded intermediates must be transferred to the bubble interface and adsorbed on it before being degraded. This may be one of the reasons why the RhB degradation rate becomes limited by the available interface area of bubbles that results in reduction of treatment efficiency at a higher concentration of RhB [40]. However, this assumption should be confirmed by additional investigations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH on RhB degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of pH on RhB degradation was investigated within a pH range from 3 to 11 at an output voltage of 25 kV. The initial concentration of RhB was 10 mg/L, and solution conductivity was 200 μS/cm. As shown in Fig. 7 , both the acid and alkali conditions give higher degradation efficiency than that at a neutral condition. According to the results of recent investigations [40,41], dissociation of RhB at −COOH sites would hinder the diffusion of RhB molecules to the bubble-liquid interface in going from acid to neutral pH, causing the degradation efficiency to decrease. On the other hand, in alkaline medium, the enhanced degradation maybe due to the change of the hydrophobic property of RhB molecular [38]. Also, OH radicals can be rapidly converted to O- in strong alkaline solutions, which could also have an effect on the degradation of RhB [3].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of solution conductivity on RhB degradation
ce:section.ce:para: Solution conductivity has a significant effect on the RhB degradation by affecting the type of plasma discharge. Fig. 8 shows results of this effect in a conductivity range of 10–400 μS/cm. The blue dash line indicates the threshold conductivity at which the spark type discharge is transformed into the spark-streamer discharge, which can be observed as fast wavy changes of voltage and current in time between spark type discharge and streamer type discharge from oscilloscope. A drastic growth in the degradation efficiency was observed as conductivity increased from 10 to 20 μS/cm, where the degradation efficiency and reaction rate constant reached their maximum values. Sun [17] has reported a similar result, showing that OH radical emission reaches its maximum at the conductivity of a solution between 10 and 80 μS/cm due to the stronger discharge and improved streamer channeling of plasma resulting in the maximum degradation efficiency and reaction rate constant. However, with further increase in conductivity, both the degradation efficiency and rate constant show a descending trend. Notice that in the high conductivity range, spark discharge cannot be generated by plasma alone, the ion flow induces conductivity current before the electric field induces polarization [37]. The streamer type discharge shows a limited removal efficiency of RhB. Besides, compared with the streamer discharge, the spark discharge provides a more reactive environment because high energy electron clusters, UV radiation, shockwaves and supercritical water condition occurs simultaneously that enhances the radical reactions and pyrolytic in and around the plasma channels [3]. Although higher conductivity results in larger discharge current causing more intense UV radiation and higher plasma temperature, these phenomena are of less importance for RhB degradation compared to the contribution of radicals. The above-mentioned decreasing trend of degradation efficiency and reaction rate constant maybe due to a higher ratio of the streamer type discharge in the total spark-streamer type discharge under higher solution conductivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of output voltage on RhB degradation
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 shows the dependence of RhB degradation efficiency on the output voltage ranged from 25 to 32 kV. Again, the blue dash line indicates the transition from spark-streamer type discharge to spark discharge when increasing the output voltage up to 32 kV. Both degradation efficiency and rate constant increased at higher output voltages. This is because higher output voltage results in the higher electron density, and produces more such radicals as OH, HO2, H2O2 [4,17], which attack RhB molecules. Also, the higher voltage output is favorable because it enhances physical effects on assisting the RhB degradation [3]. In addition, a higher degradation efficiency obtained in the output voltage from 28 to 32 kV suggests that the spark discharge provides a better degradation performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of breakdown in ACAP process
ce:section.ce:para: As explained in the Section 3.1, acoustic cavitation bubbles between the electrodes can play a role of “bridges” for plasma propagation. To examine this assumption, the following measurements were performed. Firstly, a high-speed camera was used to capture the moment of a spark discharge in ACAP process. Notice that all experiments with high-speed camera were carried out in a solution of 200 μS/cm electric conductivity and 25 kV output voltage. Under these conditions, no spark discharge can be generated when the pulse discharge alone is used. The amplitude of ultrasound vibrations was set to the minimum value of 40 μm.

As seen from Fig. 10 , moments just before and during a spark discharge could be captured. The top area of these images is black because of the huge amount of cavitation bubbles generated near the sonotrode surface. In these conditions, the back light was scattered at the bubble-liquid interface and did not reach the video camera. Red dashed circles indicate a position of a chain of formed bubbles, which were driven by acoustic streaming. It can be seen that the generated plasma channel coincides well with the position of bubble chain. Positions of collapsed bubbles in Fig. 10(d) also indicate that plasma channel was formed through these cavitation bubbles. A hemisphere at the figure bottom is the tip of needle tungsten electrode of 1 mm in diameter.

In Fig. 11 , two fine bubbles were observed near the needle electrode. Under the action of acoustic streaming, these bubbles flow downward approaching the needle electrode. Then a spark discharge occurred. In Fig. 11(d), although the lighting plasma channel was not captured, the structure and shape of the big gas void, which is probably formed as a result of water evaporation around the plasma channel soon after the discharge suggest that plasma channel was formed through these two bubbles.

Another occurrence of the plasma channel formation was observed during a bubble contact with needle electrode, as shown in Fig. 12 . Red arrows indicate the position of a relatively big bubble of 0.3–0.5 mm in diameter, which is transferred closer to the needle electrode. At the moment of bubble contact with the electrode surface, a spark discharge channel was formed. One possible explanation is that in this big bubble, accumulated charge on the bubble surface is more than that on small bubbles, as explained in part 3.1. Once this bubble attaches with electrode surface, a big gradient of electric field could occur, causing formation of spark plasma channel.

During the measurements with high speed camera, there were some spark discharges observed without bubble chain or bubble contact with electrode. Although the area near sonotrode surface was unable to capture by the high-speed camera, it is reasonable to assume that the existence of big bubbles in large amount near the sonotrode surface induced these discharges. This suggests that there is some similarity between phenomena observed in our experiments and those reported earlier on a single bubble discharge in a needle-plate system with bubble attached to a plate electrode [31,32].

Measurements by oscilloscope also support the above assumption. Fig. 13 shows the voltage and current time variations during a plasma discharge without and with acoustic cavitation when the solution conductivity and output voltage were kept constant at 20 μS/cm and 25 kV, respectively. The plasma pulse frequency was 60 and 70 Hz respectively. The streamer channel begins to propagate from one electrode at the instant when voltage and current start to increase. Then, after a period of almost constant voltage and current, sudden decrease in voltage and increase in current are observed, revealing that the discharge propagation has reached the opposite electrode, and a spark discharge occurs. Under conventional conditions without acoustic cavitation, the propagation time is approximately 7 μs, while that in the ACAP process is ranged within 1–3 μs. Figs. 13 and 14 show typical sets of records with 1.1 μs propagation time discharge. It is assumed that the existence of numerous microbubbles shortened propagation time because it is much easier for plasma propagates through the gas or vapor phase in the bubbles than through the liquid phase. Moreover, the reason for faster propagation is that in each discharge, plasma had “chosen” a different pathway to propagate, in which bubbles have different distribution and size that would affect the propagation process.

Therefore, microbubbles play a vital role in ACAP process to promote spark discharge generation in water. Furthermore, because of shorter propagation time, higher energy density could be obtained in spark discharge, which could cause more radicals to be formed resulting in improvement of the pollutant removal efficiency [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic effects of the ACAP process
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 15 compares the RhB degradation efficiency achieved with ultrasound alone, plasma alone and ACAP process when solutions of various conductivities were used. Table 1 presents the degradation rate constants. It is readily seen that degradation using plasma alone is ineffective, especially in higher conductivity solutions. Degradation rate due to ultrasound alone is much higher, and the solution conductivity showed no effects on it. More obvious synergistic effect of ACAP was observed in the 200 μS·cm−1 solution. The reason is that the plasma discharge is changed from a streamer type to a spark-streamer type yielding much more radicals and inducing more intense physical effects on the RhB dye.

Table 2 shows the number of spark discharges frequency (discharge number per second) measured under various electric conductivities of solution without Rhodamine B. These data were obtained using the same high-speed camera after recording for 3 s. Then, the number of spark discharges was counted and averaged. In plasma-alone treatment, spark discharge occurs only when the electric conductivity is below 40 μS/cm. However, in the ACAP process, the spark discharge was significantly improved, especially in solutions with electric conductivity above 20 μS/cm. The increase of spark discharge frequency allows us to explain the synergistic effect of Rhodamine B degradation in ACAP process, as showed in Fig. 10. Notice that the spark discharge frequency is decreased to about half in 200 μS/cm solution.

Besides, the ultrasound irradiation causes the diffusion layer thickness to decrease far below 1 μm, consequently increasing the possibility for radicals to contact with RhB molecules [43]. Also, acoustic streaming generated due to ultrasound energy attenuation also plays an important role in the ACAP process. Acoustic streaming enhances liquid agitation and mass transport of solutes from the liquid bulk to places where degradation reactions occur. Therefore, there is no need to use any mechanical stirring, for instance impellers, because acoustic streaming provides a sufficiently strong effect to agitate large volumes of liquid. This is another advantageous feature of the ACAP process investigated in this paper.

Acoustic cavitation assisted plasma (ACAP) was proposed as a novel AOPs technique for wastewater treatment, and degradation tests on Rhodamine B solution were performed. It was found that acoustic cavitation bubbles between electrodes serve as “bridges” for plasma propagation, facilitating its initiation and making it possible to treat wastewater with a wide range of electric conductivity and solute concentration. No significant effect of ultrasound amplitude on the treatable range was observed. Investigation by high speed camera reveals the mechanism of spark discharge generation in ACAP process, which proves the vital role of microbubbles in plasma channel.

The effects of initial concentration, pH, electric conductivity and output voltage on the degradation efficiency and reaction rate constant were investigated. Results can be summarized as follows:
•
Initial concentration has significant effect on the RhB degradation.
•
In acid (pH 3–5) or alkaline (pH 9–11) solutions, the degradation efficiency is higher than that in neutral solutions.
•
The degradation efficiency and reaction rate constant show drastic growths and reach maximum values as conductivity increased from 10 to 20 μS/cm, after which the degradation efficiency begins to decrease with the increase in conductivity.
•
Both the degradation efficiency and rate constant become higher as the output voltages increase from 25 to 32 kV.
The results reveal that the ACAP process has a synergistic effect on the wastewater treatment that results from the improved spark discharge due to acoustic cavitation bubbles.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partly sponsored by Chinese Scholarship Council and Japanese Steel Foundation for Environmental Protection Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The values of Rhodamine B degradation rate and reaction constant of experiments with various parameters were shown in Table A.1 .
